questions.prompt: Why honor the Reduce Motion accessibility setting?

choices.text: Real medical need — users with vestibular disorders (inner ear / balance system conditions) experience nausea, dizziness, and headaches from screen motion, especially parallax + dramatic zoom transitions. About 35% of adults have some level of vestibular sensitivity; not edge case. Honoring Reduce Motion: replace dramatic transitions with cross-fades, eliminate parallax, skip zoom-from-thumbnail animations. Functional animations (loading spinner, progress bar) can remain in minimal form. Implementation: `@Environment(\\.accessibilityReduceMotion) var reduceMotion` then `withAnimation(reduceMotion ? .none : .spring) { ... }` or conditionally swap transitions. Apple bakes this into many standard components (NavigationStack respects it; Sheet respects it); custom animations need explicit code. Beyond medical necessity: respecting Reduce Motion shows attention to detail; users who enable it see your app degrade gracefully; trust. App Store review increasingly checks. Cheap to implement (one environment check per animation block); meaningful for affected users; standard practice for accessibility-conscious apps.

questions.explanation: Vestibular disorders cause real nausea from motion. ~35% of adults sensitive. Honor via reduceMotion environment check.

questions.prompt: Designer wants 2-second transitions for hero photo zoom 'so it feels luxurious.' Issue?

choices.text: Too slow — feels sluggish, not luxurious. Apple's animation durations are typically 100-500ms; 2 seconds is 4-20x longer than the standard. Users wait, attention drifts, perception shifts from 'polished' to 'unresponsive.' Test on device: at 2s a transition feels like the app froze. Apple's hero animations (Photos zooming from thumbnail) typically 350-450ms with carefully tuned spring physics — short enough to feel responsive, with spring overshoot that signals 'physics-real' and registers in the visual cortex as satisfying. Slower animations don't feel more luxurious; they feel SLOWER. Real luxury comes from: smooth 60-120fps rendering, well-tuned spring physics, matched-geometry continuity (element stays as it moves), appropriate easing for the action type. Test the same hero animation at 200/350/600/1000/2000ms; users near-universally prefer 350. The 'longer feels more important' instinct is wrong; correct duration feels right; longer feels broken. Save dramatic 2s+ animations for genuinely cinematic moments (first-launch hero, occasional reward animations) — never for routine transitions.

questions.explanation: 2s feels sluggish, not luxurious. Apple norms: 100-500ms. Luxury comes from spring physics + smoothness, not duration.
